hawkish_phrases: inflation had risen sharply owing to the surge in energy prices
high inflation could persist for longer than anticipated
higher inflation in the medium term was unlikely to come about without dynamic wage growth
if inflation exceeded the target in both 2021 and 2022, this risked being perceived less and less as a temporary phenomenon
there was no need for a further loosening of the stance through additional asset purchases
dovish_phrases: monetary accommodation was still needed for inflation to stabilise at the 2% inflation target over the medium term
inflation was still projected to settle below the Governing Council's 2% target in the baseline scenario
concerns were also expressed about any premature scaling back of monetary stimulus
substantial monetary policy support was still needed
inflation was expected to remain elevated in the near term but to decline in the course of 2022
summary: The Governing Council decided to conduct net PEPP purchases at a lower pace in Q1 2022 and discontinue them at end-March, while temporarily increasing APP purchases to €40bn/month in Q2 and €30bn in Q3 before settling at €20bn from October, maintaining accommodative stance as inflation was still projected below 2% over the medium term despite near-term elevation.
